coredata.dc:title: Ruthenium nitrosyl grafted carbon dots as a fluorescence-trackable nanoplatform for visible light-controlled nitric oxide release and targeted intracellular delivery
coredata.dc:creator: Deng, Qiao
Xiang, Hui-Jing
Tang, Wei-Wei
An, Lu
Yang, Shi-Ping
Zhang, Qian-Ling
Liu, Jin-Gang
coredata.dc:description: Nitric oxide (NO) plays a key role in various physiological and pathological processes. It is of great significance in developing a platform that enables exogenous delivery of NO spatiotemporally to a targeted site for realizing NO-mediated therapy. We report herein a stable, multifunctional NO-delivery nanoplatform that is capable of target directing, fluorescence tracking, and light-controlled NO delivery. A ruthenium nitrosyl [Ru(TPYCOOH)(o-phenylenediamine)(NO)](PF6)3 and a target-directing molecule of folic acid (FA) were covalently grafted onto the surface of a carrier of carbon dots (CDs), forming a {Ru-NO@FA@CDs} nanoplatform. This nanoplatform is fluorescence self-trackable in a cellular environment and can recognize specific cancer cells via FA–folate receptor binding; it was selectively taken up by cancer cells to enter the cytosol, in which localized NO was produced on demand by adept control of visible light illumination. This offered the potential for treating diseases derived from NO deficiency as well as NO-mediated cancer therapy.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitric oxide
Photo-release
Targeted delivery
Nanoplatform
Carbon dots
Ruthenium nitrosyl

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Special Section on Clinical and Experimental Metallodrugs in Medicine

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation ruthenium nitrosyl and its grafted nanoplatform
Folate receptor-mediated target delivery of NO and its cytotoxicity toward cancer cells

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) UV–vis spectra of Ru-NO (red), and Ru-Cl (black) in DMF solution. (B) FT-IR spectra of Ru-NO (red) and Ru-Cl (black).

(A) FT-IR spectra of CDs (black), CDs-NH2 (dashed blue line), and {Ru-NO@FA@CDs} (red). (B) UV–vis spectra of CDs (black), CDs-NH2(dashed blue line), and {Ru-NO@FA@CDs} (red) in water. (C) Fluorescence spectra of CDs (black), CDs-NH2 (dashed blue line), and {Ru-NO@FA@CDs} (red) in water. Excitation wavelength: 380nm. (D) TEM image of {Ru-NO@FA@CDs} nanoplatform.

NO flux from 0.6mg/mL {Ru-NO@FA@CDs} nanoplatform dispersed in aerobic saline solution upon illumination by visible light pulses with varied intensities, (A) 100mW/cm2, (B) 200mW/cm2, and (C) 300mW/cm2. (D) Light-induced NO release from 0.6mg/mL {Ru-NO@FA@CDs} nanoplatform dispersed in anaerobic saline solution by constant light illumination (λ>400nm, 300mW/cm2).

Confocal microscopy images of HeLa (A) and A549 (B) cells treated with 50µg/mL {Ru-NO@FA@CDs} nanoplatform for 2h at 37°C. The images were obtained using an excitation wavelength of 488nm and the corresponding fluorescence was recorded between 500 and 550nm. Scale bar: 30μm.

(A) Dark and visible light-induced lethality of HeLa cells treated with different concentrations of {FA@CDs} and {Ru-NO@FA@CDs} nanoplatform (0–400µg/mL) with an incubation time of 6h. Light wavelength λ>400nm, 10min, 200mW/cm2. (B) Singlet oxygen species detection by a 1O2 trap CHDDE. Plot of CHDDE relative concentration versus irradiation time catalyzed by {FA@CDs}, and {Ru-NO@FA@CDs} nanoplatform.
Synthesis of ruthenium nitrosyl compound Ru-NO.

head.ce:title: Ruthenium nitrosyl grafted carbon dots as a fluorescence-trackable nanoplatform for visible light-controlled nitric oxide release and targeted intracellular delivery

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory for Advanced Materials of MOE, Department of Chemistry, East China University of Science and Technology, Shanghai 200237, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for Advanced Materials of MOE
East China University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Resource Chemistry of MOE, Shanghai Key Laboratory of Rare Earth Functional Materials, Shanghai Normal University, Shanghai 200234, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Resource Chemistry of MOE
Shanghai Key Laboratory of Rare Earth Functional Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shenzhen Key Laboratory of Functional Polymer, College of Chemistry and Environmental Engineering, Shenzhen University, Shenzhen 518060, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shenzhen Key Laboratory of Functional Polymer
College of Chemistry and Environmental Engineering

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Chemistry, East China University of Science and Technology, 130 Meilong Road, Shanghai 200237, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: East China University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nitric oxide (NO) plays a key role in various physiological and pathological processes. It is of great significance in developing a platform that enables exogenous delivery of NO spatiotemporally to a targeted site for realizing NO-mediated therapy. We report herein a stable, multifunctional NO-delivery nanoplatform that is capable of target directing, fluorescence tracking, and light-controlled NO delivery. A ruthenium nitrosyl [Ru(TPYCOOH)(o-phenylenediamine)(NO)](PF6)3 and a target-directing molecule of folic acid (FA) were covalently grafted onto the surface of a carrier of carbon dots (CDs), forming a {Ru-NO@FA@CDs} nanoplatform. This nanoplatform is fluorescence self-trackable in a cellular environment and can recognize specific cancer cells via FA–folate receptor binding; it was selectively taken up by cancer cells to enter the cytosol, in which localized NO was produced on demand by adept control of visible light illumination. This offered the potential for treating diseases derived from NO deficiency as well as NO-mediated cancer therapy.

A fluorescence self-trackable NO-delivery nanoplatform, in which ruthenium nitrosyl and folic acid were covalently grafted onto the surface of carbon dots, was selectively taken up by specific cancer cells to which localized NO was produced on demand by controlled visible light illumination.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Nitric oxide (NO) is an important biological signal molecule that is involved in the regulation of numerous physiological and pathophysiological processes including vasodilation, immune response, neurotransmission, respiration, and apoptosis. A decrease in the endogenous production of NO due to aging, inactivity, smoking, and poor diet has been associated with many serious medical problems such as hypertension, diabetes, liver fibrosis, cardiovascular illness, neurodegenerative diseases, and several cancers [1,2]. NO has one unpaired electron, and this free radical gas is reactive with various other molecules such as O2, superoxide, metalloenzymes and thiol groups in biological fluids. The half-life of NO in vivo is generally very short, and its survival in oxygenated media is concentration dependent [3,4]. Given that the biological functions of NO are related to its timing, location, and dosage, it is highly imperative to develop a NO-delivery nanoplatform that can release NO on demand at a desired location and time.

The biofunctionalities and promising anticancer activity of NO have thus stimulated tremendous research interest in the development of NO-releasing molecules and their related platforms. Various types of exogenous NO-donors have been prepared and applied to biological systems, including metal nitrosyls [5–9] and organic donors [10–17] such as organic nitrites (RONO) and nitrates (RONO2), diazeniumdiolates (NONOates), and S-nitrosothiols (RSNO). Compared with NO donors themselves, NO-donor-decorated nanoplatforms may increase the duration of drug circulation in the blood, allowing a reduction in the dose required to achieve therapeutic levels over an extended period of time. Organic NO donors have extensively been incorporated with various kinds of nanocarriers to provide NO delivery nanopatforms that release NO upon stimulation [11–17]. While, research involving covalent functionalizing established drug delivery systems with metal nitrosyls is very limited in the literature [18–20]. Ruthenium nitrosyl has beneficial properties relative to other NO donors: it has low cytotoxicity toward host cells, shows good stability against air oxidation, and is photolabile, facilitating immediate NO delivery upon exposure to light [7–9]. Quite recently, we reported ruthenium nitrosyl-grafted titanium dioxide nanoparticles as multifunctional NO-delivery nanoplatforms that can selectively deliver NO and singlet oxygen (1O2) simultaneously to specific cancer cell lines under total light control [19,20].

Carbon dots (CDs) have emerged as a new class of drug carriers because of their unique properties including light stability, water dispersibility (solubility), good biocompatibility, and cell permeability [21–23]. Compared with metal-containing quantum dots and nanoparticles, CDs are much safer for biological applications. Furthermore, the inherent fluorescence of CDs allows for real-time tracing of the drug delivery system in a cellular microenvironment [24]. Nanohybrids of CD-conjugated nitroaniline derivatives were recently reported to show NO release upon light irradiation [25]. Through electrostatic adsorption interactions, polyethleneimine-stabilized CDs as a photoluminescence probe, together with a photosensitive NO donor [Fe4S3(NO)7]− (Roussin's black salt), were loaded into a carboxymethyl chitosan nanosphere, which is capable of light-triggered NO release while being fluorescence monitored [26].

Currently, targeted therapy drugs [27] are attracting more and more attention since most drugs that kill cancer cells lack specificity toward tumor cells, thus producing adverse side effects in normal tissues and organs during treatment. We report herein a NO-delivery nanoplatform, {Ru-NO@FA@CDs}, in which a ruthenium nitrosyl, a Ru-NO donor, and folic acid (FA) molecules were covalently grafted onto the surface of the CDs (Scheme 1 ). The {Ru-NO@FA@CDs} nanoplatform demonstrated targeted NO delivery to specific cell lines with fluorescence tracking under total control of visible light irradiation.

All reagents were commercially purchased and used without further purification unless otherwise noted. The compounds TPYCOOH ((2,2′:6′,2″-terpyridine)-4′-carboxylic acid) and Ru(tpyCOOH)Cl3 were prepared according to the literature [28,29]. CDs were obtained according to previous reports [30,31].

1H NMR spectra were recorded on a Bruker AV 400 spectrometer (400MHz). Electrospray ionization (ESI) mass spectra were performed on a Micromass LCTTM mass spectrometer. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was performed on a JEOL JEM-2011 transmission electron microscope operating at 100kV. FTIR spectra were recorded on a Shimadzu Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (IRPrestige-21). UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded on a Shimadzu UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV-2600).

Light source: An Asahi Spectra Max 303 Xe lamp equipped with a 300–600nm UV–vis module, a 1.0 collimator lens, and a long-pass filter UV400. The intensity of visible light irradiation (λ>400nm) on the sample cell was measured using a CEL-NP 2000 intensity meter. The amount of NO released was measured using a NO-sensitive electrode (World Precision Instruments, ISONO meter, equipped with a TBR1025 free radical analyzer for measuring NO from 0.3nM to 100μM). The amperometric data collected from the electrode were then converted into the corresponding NO concentration. Fluorescence images were acquired using a Leica TCS SP5 II inverted microscope with a Leica DMI 6000B confocal scanning system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation ruthenium nitrosyl and its grafted nanoplatform

ce:section.ce:para: To a mixture of EtOH-H2O (40mL, 3:1, v/v) containing LiCl (0.085g, 2.0mmol) and Et3N (0.4mL), Ru(TPYCOOH)Cl3 (0.150g, 0.31mmol) and o-phenylenediamine (0.028g, 0.26mmol) were added. The solution mixture was heated at reflux for 6h under N2 atmosphere. The resulting clear deep-red solution was concentrated to about 10mL by rotary evaporation and refrigerated at 4°C for 10h. The precipitates were then filtered, washed with distilled water and Et2O, and dried to afford a violet-black solid (Ru-Cl). Yield: 60% (95mg). 1H NMR (400MHz, DMSO-d 6) δ 14.29 (s, 1H), 12.11 (s, 1H), 9.06 (s, 1H), 8.77 (d, 2H), 8.02 (t, 2H), 7.76 (d, 2H), 7.37 (m, 4H), 7.04 (t, 1H), and 6.94 (m, 2H) ppm. ESI-MS m/z [M-Cl]: calculated 520.0, found 520.1.

The precursor of Ru-Cl (0.156g, 0.30mmol), AgNO3 (0.506g, 2.97mmol), and CH3CN-H2O (8.0mL, 1:5, v/v) were mixed and heated to reflux for 6h under N2 atmosphere. The solution was cooled to room temperature, and the precipitated AgCl was separated by filtration through a sintered glass frit. An excess of NaNO2 (0.47g, 6.8mmol) was added to the filtrate and the mixture was heated under reflux for 2h. After cooling to room temperature, the solution was reduced to about 5.0mL under vacuum. To the solution was slowly added 4.0mL concentrated aqueous HNO3 and cooled to 273K; the solution was stirred for a further 1h. Addition of saturated aqueous NH4PF6 solution to the above mixture produced a dark brown solid. The precipitates thus obtained were filtered off, washed with water and Et2O, and then dried in vacuo. Yield: 110mg (71%). 1H NMR (400MHz, DMSO-d 6) δ 9.25 (s, 2H), 9.08 (d, 2H), 8.45 (s, 1H), 8.42 (s, 1H), 8.37 (t, 2H), 8.22 (d, 1H), 8.11 (d,1H), 7.99 (d, 2H), and 7.69 (t, 2H) ppm. ESI-MS m/z [M-2PF6 −]+: calculated 660.0, found 660.1. FTIR ν NO: 1895cm−1.

CDs were ultrasonically dispersed in deionized water to yield a 1.0mg/mL solution, and the pH of the solution was adjusted to 8.0 by addition of aqueous ammonia, followed by activation with an EDC/NHS (EDC: 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethyl aminopropyl) carbodiimide, NHS: N-hydroxysuccinimide) solution for 30min. The pH of the solution was then reduced to 6.0 by addition of aqueous HCl (0.1M), and the solution was vigorously stirred at room temperature for 4h before adding excess ethylenediamine (2.0mL). The resulting solution was further stirred at room temperature for 6h. After that, the remaining excess reagents were removed by dialysis (MWCO 1000) for 48h. The solution within the dialysis bag was freeze dried to yield the amine-passivated CDs (CDs-NH2).

Ru-NO (76mg, 0.08mmol) and FA (5.0mg, 0.01mmol) were dissolved in 5.0mL DMF and then activated by an EDC/NHS solution for 30min. Following that, 50.0mg CDs-NH2 was added to react for 24h at room temperature. The remaining unreacted reagents were removed by dialysis (MWCO 1000). The solution within the dialysis bag was freeze dried to yield the final {Ru-NO@FA@CDs} nanoplatform.

The {Ru-NO@FA@CDs} nanoplatform (0.6mg/mL) was dispersed in saline solution (150mM) in a quartz cuvette under gentle stirring with a magnetic stirring bar. NO release was initiated by irradiation of the sample cell with a 300W Xenon lamp (Asahi Spectra Max-303 equipped with a 300–600nm UV–vis module, a 1.0 collimator lens, and a long-pass filter, UV400). The amount of NO released was measured using a NO-sensitive electrode. The tip of the electrode was placed outside the light path. The electrode was accurately calibrated by mixing standard solutions of NaNO2 with H2SO4 (0.1M) and KI (0.1M), according to the protocol indicated in the manufacturer's manual. The amperometric data collected from the electrode were then converted to the corresponding NO concentration.

LED sources of blue (470nm) and green (530nm) light were used for NO quantum yield measurement. The light intensity was determined before each photolysis experiment using an actinometer (measured intensity of ~5mW). The solution of {Ru-NO@FA@CDs} was placed in a 1.0-cm pathlength quartz cuvette, 1.0cm away from the light source. The solution was prepared to ensure sufficient absorbance (>90%) at the irradiation wavelength and was agitated periodically under an argon atmosphere during the photolysis experiment. NO quantum yields (Φt) were calculated from NO concentrations, obtained by NO meter measurement. The calculated values were plotted against t. These plots were linear with a negative slope for the first 20%–25% of the reaction. The extrapolated quantum yield at t =0 (y intercept) was taken as ΦNO for the photolabilization of NO from the {Ru-NO@FA@CDs} solution.

Human cervical carcinoma cells (HeLa cells) and human lung cancer cells (A549 cells) were obtained from the Shanghai Institutes for Biological Sciences (SIBS), Chinese Academy of Science (CAS, China). HeLa cells and A549 cells were cultured in Roswell Park Memorial Institute medium (RPMI-1640, Thermo, USA) and Dulbecco's Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM, Thermo, USA) at 37°C under 5% CO2 atmosphere, supplemented with 10% (v/v) fetal bovine serum (Gibco, USA) and 1% (v/v) penicillin/streptomycin.

All the cells were seeded in a 96-well plate with a density of 5×104 cells per well and were incubated in a humidified 5% CO2 atmosphere for 24h. The cell culture medium was removed and washed with phosphate buffer solution (PBS). Following that, different concentrations of the {Ru-NO@FA@CDs} nanoplatform (0, 50, 100, 200, and 400μg/mL) in cell culture medium were added and incubated further for a period of 12 or 24h at 37°C in a humidified 5% CO2 atmosphere. MTT (20μL, 5.0mg/mL) solution was added to each well. After 4h of incubation at 37°C, the cell culture medium was removed and formazan crystals were lysed with 150μL DMSO. The absorbance was then measured at 490nm using a microplate reader (Multiskan MK3, USA).

Visible light irradiation experiments: After incubation of cells with different concentrations of the {Ru-NO@FA@CDs} nanoplatform (0, 50, 100, 200, and 400μg/mL) for 4h, light irradiation was applied (λ>400nm, 200mW/cm2, 10min) and the cells were incubated for 6h.

Fluorescence imaging was performed with a Leica DMI 6000B confocal scanning system. A 488-nm laser was used as the excitation source and the corresponding emissions were collected in the wavelength range 500–550nm. HeLa and A549 cells were seeded on a plastic-bottomed μ-dish of diameter 35mm, with a density of 104 cells, and maintained at 37°C in 5% CO2 atmosphere for 24h. The cells were then treated with the {Ru-NO@FA@CDs} solution (100μg/mL) for 6h. After incubation, the cells were washed twice with PBS and subjected to confocal fluorescence microscopy analysis.

The UV–vis absorption spectrum of {Ru-NO@FA@CDs} was dominated by the absorption of CDs, where a weak, broad absorption band was observed at ~275nm (Fig. 2B). This band was approximately equal to the electronic absorption band of FA (λmax: 280nm) [24], indicating the successful anchoring of FA to the {Ru-NO@FA@CDs} nanoplatform. In addition, the electronic absorption of {Ru-NO@FA@CDs} in the visible region is much enhanced compared with that of the bare CDs when Ru-NO was covalently grafted (Fig. 2B). This is beneficial as it allows the use of longer wavelength light, rather than the UV light usually employed for ruthenium nitrosyls to trigger NO release. The fluorescence spectra of {Ru-NO@FA@CDs} in water exhibited a significantly reduced fluorescence intensity centered at ~495nm when compared with that of the bare CDs (Fig. 2C). The morphology of {Ru-NO@FA@CDs}, as analyzed using a transmission electron microscope, revealed globular-shaped particles with an average size of ~6.0nm (Fig. 2D). The composition of {Ru-NO@FA@CDs} was analyzed by energy dispersive spectrometry and the amount of Ru-NO incorporated onto the surface of the CDs was determined to be 8.86wt.% Ru, corresponding to ~0.88μmol/mg of loaded NO for the {Ru-NO@FA@CDs} nanoplatform.

Light-triggered NO delivery by the nanoplatform was evaluated in saline solution. NO released in solution was measured with an ISONO meter, which allows direct amperometric detection of NO with a quick response (<5s) and high sensitivity (1nM). In a typical process, incremental visible light pulses illuminate the {Ru-NO@FA@CDs} sample dispersed in saline solution resulting in the immediate generation of NO in solution (Fig. 3 ). The amount of NO released in this process was proportional to the intensity of the applied light (100–300mW/cm2) and the duration (5–20s) of light exposure (Fig. 3A–C). Under constant illumination of 0.6mg/mL of {Ru-NO@FA@CDs} in saline solution by visible light (300mW/cm2), a steady concentration of ~8μM of NO was observed (Fig. 3D). Consequently, these results indicated that the dosage of NO released from the {Ru-NO@FA@CDs} nanoplatform can be tuned by control of the exposure to visible light.

Based on the NO concentration measured by a NO-sensitive electrode in aqueous solution, the NO quantum yield (Φ) was obtained following a previously reported method [32,33]. We employed blue (470nm) and green (530nm) light for excitation and Φ values of 0.268±0.03 and 0.151±0.02moleinsten−1 were obtained, respectively. Blue light (470nm) excitation produced a larger Φ value than the green (530nm) excitation wavelength, which is consistent with the nanoplatform electronic absorption in the visible region.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Folate receptor-mediated target delivery of NO and its cytotoxicity toward cancer cells
ce:section.ce:para: Folate receptors (FRs) are potential markers for carcinomas. FA has a high affinity for FRs and is thus a typical cell-targeting agent [34–36]. To prove the feasibility of FR-mediated targeting, we employed FR-positive [FR(+)] human cervical HeLa cells and FR-negative [FR(−)] human breast A549 tumor cells as a control [37]. We verified the selective binding of {Ru-NO@FA@CDs} to the targeted HeLa cells using confocal laser scanning microscopy. Confocal images of the {Ru-NO@FA@CDs} internalized inside the cells were obtained after incubation with the cancer cell lines. The internalization of {Ru-NO@FA@CDs} in cells was readily recognized by its green fluorescence. The nanoplatform was found to localize mainly in the cytosol after 30min of incubation at 37°C. Fig. 4A depicts the selective accumulation of the {Ru-NO@FA@CDs} nanoplatform in the FR(+) HeLa cells, whereas its uptake in the FR(−) A549 cells was much less (Fig. 4B). The FR(−)A549 cells showed weak fluorescence above the background, suggesting a weak cellular association with the {Ru-NO@FA@CDs} nanoplatform and accordingly a reduced cellular uptake of the nanoplatform.

The cytotoxic effects of {Ru-NO@FA@CDs} toward the FR(+) HeLa cell lines were investigated at various concentrations ranging from 50 to 400µg/mL using MTT assay. As shown in Fig. 5 , the nanoplatform did not exhibit cytotoxic effects for any of the concentrations tested in the dark, where the cell viability was above 90%. However, the viability of HeLa cells showed a significant decrease once they were illuminated by visible light (>400nm, 200mW/cm2, 10min). The cell survival was inversely related to the concentration of the {Ru-NO@FA@CDs} nanoplatform. In a control experiment under similar experimental conditions where the {Ru-NO@FA@CDs} nanoplatform was absent, visible light illumination did not lead to cell lethality (concentration=0, Fig. 5A). Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and N, S-doped GQDs have been reported to produce reactive oxygen species under visible light irradiation which resulted in cytotoxicity [38,39]. In order to investigate the possible cytotoxicity contribution from the CDs in the {Ru-NO@FA@CDs} nanoplatform, we carried out further control experiment where sample of {FA@CDs} lacking of the ruthenium nitrosyl showed no cytotoxicity in the presence of visible light irradiation under the similar experimental conditions as that of {Ru-NO@FA@CDs} (Fig. 5A). This was further verified using a 1O2 trap of sodium 1, 3-cyclohexadiene-1, 4-diethanoate (CHDDE) [19]. Both {FA@CDs} and {Ru-NO@FA@CDs} produced no singlet oxygen species under visible light illumination (Fig. 5B). Taken together, these results strongly suggested that the cytotoxicity effect associated with the {Ru-NO@FA@CDs} nanoplatform did not result from the light itself or from the CDs, but from the released NO.

In summary, we developed a multifunctional NO-delivery nanoplatform in which ruthenium nitrosyls, together with cancer-cell-directing FA molecules, were covalently grafted onto the surface of a carrier of CDs. The {Ru-NO@FA@CDs} nanoplatform is capable of preferentially targeting RF-overexpressed cancer cell lines and subsequently enabling light-controlled intracellular delivery of NO to kill cancer cells. The inherent fluorescence of CDs allowed the {Ru-NO@FA@CDs} nanoplatform to be fluorescence-tracked while it was being taken up and internalized by cells. Moreover, the amount of NO released from the {Ru-NO@FA@CDs} nanoplatform can be controlled by manipulating its exposure to visible light, allowing spatiotemporal delivery of NO with concentrations varying from nM to μM. This offered the potential to be used in the treatment of diseases derived from NO deficiency as well as NO-mediated cancer therapy.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was financially supported by the NSF of China (no. 21271072, 21571062 to JGL; 21471101 to QLZ), the Program for Professor of Special Appointment (Eastern Scholar) at Shanghai Institutions of Higher Learning, the Shanghai Pujiang Program (no. 13J1401900) to JGL, and Shenzhen Government's Plan of Science and Technology (JCY20150324140036843) to QLZ. We thank the National Institutes of Health and National Cancer Institute for grant 1R13CA200223-01A1 (Conference Organization support, 1st International Symposium on Clinical and Experimental Metallodrugs in Medicine: Cancer Chemotherapy, CEMM). Abbreviations CDs: carbon dots DMEM: Dulbecco's Modified Eagle Medium EDC: 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethyl aminopropyl) carbodiimide EDS: energy dispersive spectroscopy FA: folic acid FR: folate receptor NHS: N-hydroxysuccinimide MTT: 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyl tetrazolium bromide NO: nitric oxide PBS: phosphate buffer solution RPMI-1640: Roswell Park Memorial Institute medium TEM: transmission electron microscopy TPYCOOH: (2,2′:6′,2″-terpyridine)-4′-carboxylic acid
